reasoning: Q6 is a highly selective single-table scan (2.4% selectivity) on 60M rows with simple aggregation. GenDB is already competitive with DuckDB (29.3ms vs 20ms). With 64 cores + AVX512, aggressive parallelism and SIMD filtering should close the gap. Zone maps on sorted l_shipdate enable efficient block skipping.
focus_areas: Maximize parallelism: 64 threads with morsel-driven execution (~10K rows/morsel)
Zone map exploitation: skip blocks outside date/quantity/discount ranges using existing indexes
SIMD filtering: AVX512 vectorized predicate evaluation for l_shipdate, l_discount, l_quantity
Partial aggregation: thread-local accumulation, then lock-free merge to minimize contention
Column pruning: only load l_shipdate, l_discount, l_quantity, l_extendedprice (4/16 columns)
Predicate ordering: evaluate cheapest predicates first (l_quantity < 24, then l_discount BETWEEN, then l_shipdate range)
Branch-free filtering: use bitmasks to reduce branch mispredictions in tight scan loop
Memory access pattern: sequential scan within blocks for cache efficiency
strategy_notes: This is a best-case workload for columnar + parallel execution. The 2.4% selectivity means ~1.4M qualifying rows out of 60M. With zone maps on sorted l_shipdate data, expect to skip 80%+ of blocks immediately. The Query Optimizer should generate a parallel scan with: (1) Zone map pruning at block level, (2) SIMD-accelerated predicate evaluation within blocks, (3) Thread-local partial sums, (4) Final merge. Target near-linear speedup with 64 cores. The storage is already optimized (columnar, sorted, zone maps) so focus on execution efficiency.
notes: GenDB is already very close to DuckDB (29.3ms vs 20ms target). The goal is to match or beat DuckDB by exploiting the 64-core hardware and AVX512. Be aggressive with parallelism and SIMD — this query has no complex logic to worry about.
